critique.summary: The article introduces Attention Residuals (AttnRes), a novel modification to standard residual connections in Transformer models that uses learned, input-dependent attention over previous layer outputs to improve performance and training dynamics. It presents a variant called Block AttnRes to reduce memory usage and shows empirical results indicating performance gains on various benchmarks and better gradient distribution during training.
mainPoints: Standard residual connections in Transformers accumulate all layer outputs with fixed weights, which can dilute contributions and cause unbounded growth in hidden-state magnitudes.
AttnRes replaces fixed accumulation with softmax attention over preceding layer outputs, allowing selective, content-aware aggregation of earlier representations.
Block AttnRes partitions layers into blocks to reduce memory complexity while maintaining most benefits of full AttnRes.
Empirical results show AttnRes improves performance across multiple benchmarks including MMLU, GPQA-Diamond, BBH, TriviaQA, and code generation tasks.
AttnRes mitigates PreNorm dilution by keeping output magnitudes bounded and distributing gradient norms more uniformly across layers.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article align with known issues in Transformer architectures regarding residual connections and gradient flow, and the proposed solution of using attention over residuals is a plausible and novel approach. The repository includes a paper on arXiv (arXiv:2603.15031) which supports the technical details and experimental results presented. The reported benchmark improvements and training dynamics are consistent with the methodology described, though independent verification from multiple sources or peer-reviewed publications would strengthen confidence. Overall, the information appears accurate and credible based on the available technical documentation and results.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential computational overhead or latency impacts of AttnRes in real-world deployment scenarios.
There is no mention of how AttnRes performs on tasks outside the reported benchmarks or with different model architectures.
The long-term stability and generalization of models trained with AttnRes under diverse conditions remain unaddressed.
The repository and paper appear to come from a research team but lack extensive third-party validation or citations from other independent research groups.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the concept of Attention Residuals, a novel approach to residual connections in neural networks that uses attention mechanisms to selectively aggregate layer outputs. Commenters highlight its potential benefits in reducing compute and memory requirements, practical implementation details, and its innovative nature, with some expressing curiosity about its effects on gradient behavior.

comments.author: jryio
comments.text:
This is the key piece

> Full AttnRes is straightforward but requires O(Ld) memory at scale. Block AttnRes partitions layers into N blocks, accumulates within each block via standard residuals, and applies attention only over block-level representations. With ~8 blocks, it recovers most of Full AttnRes's gains while serving as a practical drop-in replacement with marginal overhead.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately summarizes a key technical detail from the paper, explaining the trade-off between full and block attention residuals and their practical implications.

comments.author: jjcm
comments.text:
Two things stand out to me with this:

1. Drops compute required for training by ~20%. This approach wont just help the ever escalating model sizes larger companies are pushing for, it means things like autoresearch can iterate on new model architectures faster.

2. WAY lower bandwidth requirements for inference. Means with approaches like this it should run on consumer hardware far better. It apparently requires 1/6th the memory bandwidth of a traditional approach for better results.

This is a big improvement if it can be generalized. They're claiming it's a drop in replacement, so it seems like it can as well.
comments.analysis: The comment highlights important practical benefits claimed by the paper, reflecting a good understanding of the impact on training and inference efficiency.

comments.author: westurner
comments.text:
ScholarlyArticle: "Attention Residuals" (2026) https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15031 :

> Abstract: Residual connections with PreNorm are standard in modern LLMs, yet they accumulate all layer outputs with fixed unit weights. This uniform aggregation causes uncontrolled hidden-state growth with depth, progressively diluting each layer's contribution. We propose Attention Residuals (AttnRes), which replaces this fixed accumulation with softmax attention over preceding layer outputs, allowing each layer to selectively aggregate earlier representations with learned, input-dependent weights. To address the memory and communication overhead of attending over all preceding layer outputs for large-scale model training, we introduce Block AttnRes, which partitions layers into blocks and attends over block-level representations, reducing the memory footprint while preserving most of the gains of full AttnRes. [...]
comments.analysis: This comment provides a direct quote from the abstract, accurately capturing the motivation and technical approach of Attention Residuals.

comments.author: roger_
comments.text:
Great idea and seems quite obvious in hindsight.

Is it guaranteed to have the same effect on vanishing gradients though? What if it put weight 1 on a layer that had a tiny gradient?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid and insightful question about the potential impact of attention residuals on gradient flow, showing critical engagement with the concept.

comments.author: imtringued
comments.text:
I don't know why all the posts fail to summarize the results properly.

I had a similar idea at the back of my head but here is a layman explanation:

Standard attention threads the previous layers output to the next layers input. By adding residual connections to each layer, the layers learn an update rule.

There is an obvious limitation here. Only the first layer gets to see the original input and all subsequent layers only get to see the previous layer output.

With attention residuals, the idea is that you have a tiny attention operator that decides between using the original input and any of the previous layer outputs.
comments.analysis: Provides a layman explanation that clarifies the conceptual difference between standard residuals and attention residuals, aiding comprehension.

comments.author: jszymborski
comments.text: This is reminds me of the input gates of an LSTM.
comments.analysis: Makes an analogy to LSTM input gates, which is a reasonable comparison but lacks elaboration or direct evidence.

comments.author: Murfalo
comments.text: Amazingly, the first author is a high school student! https://nathanchen.me/public/About%20me.html
comments.analysis: Provides an interesting personal fact about the author but does not contribute to the technical discussion.

comments.author: caderosche
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm but adds no substantive content or analysis.

comments.author: ilreb
comments.analysis: Simply points out a duplicate link without adding new information.
